    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 95
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/95](https://www.erdosproblems.com/95)
    23→
    24→For $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ with distance multiplicities $f(u_i)$, the sum
    25→$\sum_i f(u_i)^2 \ll_\varepsilon n^{3+\varepsilon}$ for every $\varepsilon > 0$.
    26→Proved by Guth and Katz (2015) with the stronger bound $O(n^3 \log n)$.
    27→This problem carried a $500 prize. See also Problem 94.
    28→
    29→[Er92e] Erdős, P., _Some unsolved problems in geometry, number theory and combinatorics_.
    30→Eureka (1992), 44–48.
    31→
    32→[Er95] Erdős, P., _Some of my favourite problems in various branches of combinatorics_.
    33→Combinatorics '94 (Catania), Congressus Numerantium 107 (1995).
    34→
    35→[Er97c] Erdős, P., _Some recent problems and results in graph theory_. Discrete Math.
    36→**164** (1997), 81–85.
    37→
    38→[Er97f] Erdős, P., _Some of my new and almost new problems and results in combinatorial
    39→geometry_. (1997)
    40→
    41→[Al63] Altman, E., _On a problem of P. Erdős_. Amer. Math. Monthly 70 (1963), 148–157.
    42→
    43→[GuKa15] Guth, L. and Katz, N.H., _On the Erdős distinct distances problem in the plane_.
    44→Ann. of Math. (2) **181** (2015), 155–190.
    45→-/
    46→
    47→open scoped Classical
    48→
    49→namespace Erdos95
    50→
    51→/--
    52→The set of distinct distances determined by a finite point set $P$ in $\mathbb{R}^2$.
    53→-/
    54→noncomputable def distinctDistances (P : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) : Finset ℝ :=
    55→  ((P ×ˢ P).filter (fun pq => pq.1 ≠ pq.2)).image (fun pq => dist pq.1 pq.2)
    56→
    57→/--
    58→The number of ordered pairs $(p, q)$ from $P$ with $p \neq q$ and $\operatorname{dist}(p, q) = d$.
    59→-/
    60→noncomputable def distMultiplicity (P : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) (d : ℝ) : ℕ :=
    61→  ((P ×ˢ P).filter (fun pq => pq.1 ≠ pq.2 ∧ dist pq.1 pq.2 = d)).card
    62→
    63→/--
    64→Let $x_1, \ldots, x_n \in \mathbb{R}^2$ determine the set of distances $\{u_1, \ldots, u_t\}$.
    65→Suppose $u_i$ appears as the distance between $f(u_i)$ many pairs of points. Then for all
    66→$\varepsilon > 0$,
    67→$$\sum_i f(u_i)^2 \ll_\varepsilon n^{3+\varepsilon}.$$
    68→
    69→This was proved by Guth and Katz (2015) who showed the stronger bound
    70→$\sum_i f(u_i)^2 \ll n^3 \log n$.
    71→-/
    72→@[category research solved, AMS 5 52]
    73→theorem erdos_95 :
    74→    ∀ ε : ℝ, ε > 0 →
    75→      ∃ C : ℝ, C > 0 ∧
    76→        ∀ (P : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))),
    77→          ((distinctDistances P).sum
    78→            (fun d => (distMultiplicity P d) ^ 2) : ℝ) ≤
    79→            C * (P.card : ℝ) ^ ((3 : ℝ) + ε) := by
    80→  sorry
    81→
    82→end Erdos95
    83→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 98
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/98](https://www.erdosproblems.com/98)
    23→
    24→Let $h(n)$ be the minimum number of distinct distances determined by $n$ points in
    25→$\mathbb{R}^2$ with no three collinear and no four concyclic. Does $h(n)/n \to \infty$?
    26→
    27→[Er75f] Erdős, P., _Problems and results in combinatorial geometry_, 1975, p. 101.
    28→
    29→[Er83c] Erdős, P., _Old and new problems in combinatorial analysis and graph theory_, 1983.
    30→
    31→[Er87b] Erdős, P., _Some combinatorial and metric problems in geometry_. Intuitive geometry
    32→(Siófok, 1985) (1987), 167-177.
    33→
    34→[Er90] Erdős, P., _Some of my favourite unsolved problems_. A tribute to Paul Erdős (1990),
    35→467-478.
    36→
    37→[Er92b] Erdős, P., _Some of my old and new problems in elementary number theory and
    38→geometry_. Proceedings of the Fifth Canadian Number Theory Association Conference (1992).
    39→
    40→[EFPR93] Erdős, P., Füredi, Z., Pach, J., and Ruzsa, I. Z., _The grid revisited_.
    41→Discrete Mathematics (1993), 189-196.
    42→
    43→[Er94b] Erdős, P., _Some old and new problems in various branches of combinatorics_.
    44→Discrete Math. 165/166 (1997), 227-231.
    45→
    46→[Er97e] Erdős, P., _Some problems and results on combinatorial number theory_ (1997).
    47→-/
    48→
    49→open EuclideanGeometry
    50→
    51→namespace Erdos98
    52→
    53→/--
    54→A finite point set in $\mathbb{R}^2$ has no three collinear if every three-element subset
    55→is not collinear (i.e., no line contains three or more of the points).
    56→-/
    57→def NoThreeCollinear (P : Finset (ℝ²)) : Prop :=
    58→  ∀ S : Finset (ℝ²),
    59→    S ⊆ P → S.card = 3 → ¬Collinear ℝ (S : Set (ℝ²))
    60→
    61→/--
    62→Four points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ are concyclic if they all lie on a common circle, i.e.,
    63→there exists a center and positive radius such that all four points are
    64→equidistant from the center.
    65→-/
    66→def FourPointsConcyclic (S : Finset (ℝ²)) : Prop :=
    67→  ∃ c : ℝ², ∃ r : ℝ, r > 0 ∧ ∀ p ∈ S, dist p c = r
    68→
    69→/--
    70→A finite point set in $\mathbb{R}^2$ has no four concyclic if every four-element subset
    71→does not lie on a common circle.
    72→-/
    73→def NoFourConcyclic (P : Finset (ℝ²)) : Prop :=
    74→  ∀ S : Finset (ℝ²),
    75→    S ⊆ P → S.card = 4 → ¬FourPointsConcyclic S
    76→
    77→/--
    78→Erdős Problem 98:
    79→Let $h(n)$ be the minimum number of distinct distances determined by any $n$ points
    80→in $\mathbb{R}^2$ with no three collinear and no four concyclic. Does $h(n)/n \to \infty$?
    81→
    82→Formally: for every $C > 0$ there exists $N$ such that for all $n \geq N$ and every
    83→set $P$ of $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ with no three collinear and no four concyclic,
    84→the number of distinct distances is at least $C \cdot n$.
    85→-/
    86→@[category research open, AMS 5 52]
    87→theorem erdos_98 : answer(sorry) ↔
    88→    ∀ C : ℝ, C > 0 →
    89→      ∃ N : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, n ≥ N →
    90→        ∀ P : Finset ℝ²,
    91→          P.card = n →
    92→          NoThreeCollinear P →
    93→          NoFourConcyclic P →
    94→          (distinctDistances P : ℝ) ≥ C * (n : ℝ) := by
    95→  sorry
    96→
    97→end Erdos98
    98→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 102
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/102](https://www.erdosproblems.com/102)
    23→
    24→Erdős–Purdy conjecture: if an $n$-point set in $\mathbb{R}^2$ determines at least $cn^2$ lines
    25→each containing more than three points, then some line must contain an unbounded number of
    26→points as $n \to \infty$.
    27→
    28→It is not even known whether $h_c(n) \geq 5$ (see Problem 101).
    29→
    30→Erdős originally conjectured the stronger bound $h_c(n) \gg_c n^{1/2}$, but this was disproven
    31→by Hunter using point sets in $\{1, \ldots, m\}^d$ projected to $\mathbb{R}^2$, which shows
    32→$h_c(n) \ll n^{1/\log(1/c)}$. It is easy to see that $h_c(n) \ll_c n^{1/2}$.
    33→
    34→[Er92e] Erdős, P., _Some unsolved problems in geometry, number theory and combinatorics_.
    35→Eureka (1992), 44-48.
    36→
    37→[Er95] Erdős, P., _Some of my favourite problems in various branches of combinatorics_.
    38→Congressus Numerantium 107 (1995).
    39→
    40→[Er97c] Erdős, P., _Some recent problems and results in graph theory_. Discrete Math.
    41→**164** (1997), 81–85.
    42→-/
    43→
    44→namespace Erdos102
    45→
    46→/--
    47→The number of distinct affine lines in $\mathbb{R}^2$ that contain at least $4$ points from $P$
    48→(i.e., more than $3$ points, as in the problem statement).
    49→-/
    50→noncomputable def fourPlusRichLineCount (P : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) : ℕ :=
    51→  Set.ncard {L : AffineSubspace ℝ (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)) |
    52→    Module.finrank ℝ L.direction = 1 ∧
    53→    4 ≤ Set.ncard {p : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2) | p ∈ (P : Set _) ∧ p ∈ L}}
    54→
    55→/--
    56→Erdős Problem 102 (Erdős–Purdy):
    57→
    58→For fixed $c > 0$, let $h_c(n)$ denote the largest integer $h$ such that: for every
    59→$n$-point set $P$ in $\mathbb{R}^2$ with at least $c \cdot n^2$ lines each containing more than $3$ points
    60→of $P$, some line contains at least $h$ points of $P$. (In other words, $h_c(n)$ is the
    61→minimum, over all such configurations $P$, of the maximum collinearity.)
    62→
    63→The conjecture is that $h_c(n) \to \infty$ as $n \to \infty$, i.e., for each fixed $c > 0$ and
    64→each $M \in \mathbb{N}$, there exists $N$ such that for all $n \geq N$, every $n$-point configuration
    65→with at least $c \cdot n^2$ four-rich lines must contain some line with at least $M$ points.
    66→-/
    67→@[category research open, AMS 5 52]
    68→theorem erdos_102 :
    69→  ∀ c : ℝ, c > 0 →
    70→    ∀ M : ℕ,
    71→      ∃ N : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, n ≥ N →
    72→        ∀ P : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)),
    73→          P.card = n →
    74→          c * (n : ℝ) ^ 2 ≤ (fourPlusRichLineCount P : ℝ) →
    75→          ∃ L : AffineSubspace ℝ (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)),
    76→            Module.finrank ℝ L.direction = 1 ∧
    77→            M ≤ Set.ncard {p : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2) | p ∈ (P : Set _) ∧ p ∈ L} := by
    78→  sorry
    79→
    80→/--
    81→Erdős Problem 102 (stronger conjecture, disproven):
    82→
    83→Erdős originally conjectured that $h_c(n) \gg_c n^{1/2}$, i.e., for each fixed $c > 0$ there
    84→exists a constant $C > 0$ such that for all sufficiently large $n$, every $n$-point configuration
    85→in $\mathbb{R}^2$ with at least $c \cdot n^2$ four-rich lines must contain some line with at least
    86→$C \cdot n^{1/2}$ points. This was disproven by Zach Hunter, who showed that point sets in
    87→$\{1, \ldots, m\}^d$ projected to $\mathbb{R}^2$ yield $h_c(n) \ll n^{1/\log(1/c)}$.
    88→-/
    89→@[category research solved, AMS 5 52]
    90→theorem erdos_102_strong_conjecture_false :
    91→    ¬ (∀ c : ℝ, c > 0 →
    92→      ∃ C : ℝ, C > 0 ∧
    93→        ∃ N : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, n ≥ N →
    94→          ∀ P : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)),
    95→            P.card = n →
    96→            c * (n : ℝ) ^ 2 ≤ (fourPlusRichLineCount P : ℝ) →
    97→            ∃ L : AffineSubspace ℝ (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)),
    98→              Module.finrank ℝ L.direction = 1 ∧
    99→              C * (n : ℝ) ^ (1/2 : ℝ) ≤ Set.ncard {p : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2) | p ∈ (P : Set _) ∧ p ∈ L}) := by
   100→  sorry
   101→
   102→end Erdos102
   103→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 105
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/105](https://www.erdosproblems.com/105)
    23→
    24→Erdős–Purdy conjecture (disproved, $50 prize): given $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$, not all
    25→collinear, and a disjoint set of $n - 3$ points, must there exist a line through at least two
    26→of the first set that avoids the second? Counterexamples were found by Xichuan.
    27→
    28→Beck [Be83] and Szemerédi–Trotter [SzTr83] proved the analogous statement holds when
    29→$|B| = cn$ for a constant $c > 0$ (see Problem #211).
    30→
    31→[Er95] Erdős, P., _Some of my favourite problems in various branches of combinatorics_.
    32→Combinatorics '94 (Catania), Congressus Numerantium 107 (1995).
    33→
    34→[ErPu95] Erdős, P. and Purdy, G., _Two combinatorial problems in the plane_.
    35→Discrete & Computational Geometry (1995), 441–443.
    36→
    37→[Be83] Beck, J., _On the lattice property of the plane and some problems of Dirac,
    38→Motzkin and Erdős in combinatorial geometry_. Combinatorica 3 (1983), 281–297.
    39→
    40→[SzTr83] Szemerédi, E. and Trotter, W.T., _Extremal problems in discrete geometry_.
    41→Combinatorica 3 (1983), 381–392.
    42→-/
    43→
    44→namespace Erdos105
    45→
    46→/--
    47→Erdős Problem \#105 (Erdős–Purdy):
    48→Let $A, B \subset \mathbb{R}^2$ be disjoint finite sets with $|A| = n$ and $|B| = n - 3$,
    49→with not all of $A$ contained on a single line. The conjecture asks whether
    50→there must exist a line containing at least two points from $A$ and no points
    51→from $B$.
    52→
    53→Note: This conjecture has been disproved. Xichuan found three explicit
    54→counterexamples. It remains open whether the result holds with $n - 4$ (or
    55→more generally with $n - O(1)$ or $(1 - o(1))n$ points in $B$).
    56→The condition $n - 2$ is known to fail via a construction of Hickerson.
    57→-/
    58→@[category research solved, AMS 5 52]
    59→theorem erdos_105 : answer(False) ↔
    60→    ∀ n : ℕ, 3 ≤ n →
    61→    ∀ A B : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)),
    62→      A.card = n →
    63→      B.card = n - 3 →
    64→      Disjoint A B →
    65→      ¬ Collinear ℝ (A : Set (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) →
    66→      ∃ L : AffineSubspace ℝ (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)),
    67→        Module.finrank ℝ L.direction = 1 ∧
    68→        2 ≤ Set.ncard {p : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2) | p ∈ (A : Set _) ∧ p ∈ L} ∧
    69→        ∀ p : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2), p ∈ (B : Set _) → p ∉ L := by
    70→  sorry
    71→
    72→end Erdos105
    73→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 110
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/110](https://www.erdosproblems.com/110)
    23→
    24→Is there a function $F$ such that every graph with chromatic number $\aleph_1$ has, for all
    25→large $n$, a subgraph with chromatic number $n$ on at most $F(n)$ vertices? Disproved:
    26→Lambie-Hanson constructed a ZFC counterexample.
    27→
    28→[dBEr51] de Bruijn, N. G. and Erdős, P., _A colour problem for infinite graphs and a problem in
    29→the theory of relations_. Indag. Math. (1951), 369–373.
    30→
    31→[EHS82] Erdős, P., Hajnal, A., and Szemerédi, E., _On almost bipartite large chromatic graphs_.
    32→Theory and Practice of Combinatorics (1982), 117–123.
    33→
    34→[Er95d] Erdős, P., _On some problems in combinatorial set theory_. Publ. Inst. Math. (Beograd)
    35→(N.S.) (1995), 61–65.
    36→
    37→[KoSh05] Komjáth, P. and Shelah, S., _Finite subgraphs of uncountably chromatic graphs_.
    38→J. Graph Theory (2005), 28–38.
    39→
    40→[La20] Lambie-Hanson, C., _On the growth rate of chromatic numbers of finite subgraphs_.
    41→Advances in Mathematics (2020), 107176.
    42→-/
    43→
    44→open SimpleGraph Cardinal
    45→
    46→namespace Erdos110
    47→
    48→/--
    49→Erdős-Hajnal-Szemerédi Conjecture (Problem #110):
    50→Is there some function $F : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ such that every graph $G$ with chromatic
    51→number $\aleph_1$ has, for all sufficiently large $n$, a subgraph with chromatic number $n$ on at
    52→most $F(n)$ vertices?
    53→
    54→Conjectured by Erdős, Hajnal, and Szemerédi [EHS82].
    55→The analogous statement fails for graphs of chromatic number $\aleph_0$.
    56→Shelah [KoSh05] proved it is consistent with ZFC that the answer is no.
    57→Lambie-Hanson [La20] constructed a ZFC counterexample, so the conjecture is **false**.
    58→-/
    59→@[category research solved, AMS 3 5]
    60→theorem erdos_110 : answer(False) ↔
    61→    ∃ F : ℕ → ℕ,
    62→      ∀ (V : Type*) (G : SimpleGraph V),
    63→        G.chromaticCardinal = aleph 1 →
    64→        ∃ N : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, N ≤ n →
    65→          ∃ H : G.Subgraph,
    66→            H.verts.Finite ∧
    67→            H.verts.ncard ≤ F n ∧
    68→            H.coe.chromaticNumber = ↑n := by
    69→  sorry
    70→
    71→end Erdos110
    72→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 115
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/115](https://www.erdosproblems.com/115)
    23→
    24→If the lemniscate $\{z : |p(z)| \leq 1\}$ of a degree-$n$ polynomial is connected, then
    25→$|p'(z)| \leq (1/2 + o(1)) n^2$ on it. Proved by Eremenko and Lempert.
    26→
    27→[Er61, Er90] Early formulations by Erdős.
    28→[ErLe94] Eremenko, A. and Lempert, L., _An extremal problem for polynomials_.
    29→  Proc. Amer. Math. Soc. 122 (1994), 191–193.
    30→[Po59a] Pommerenke, Ch., _On the derivative of a polynomial_.
    31→  Michigan Math. J. 6 (1959), 373–375.
    32→-/
    33→
    34→open Polynomial
    35→
    36→namespace Erdos115
    37→
    38→/-- The lemniscate of a complex polynomial $p$: the sublevel set
    39→$\{z \in \mathbb{C} : |p(z)| \leq 1\}$. -/
    40→def lemniscate (p : Polynomial ℂ) : Set ℂ :=
    41→  {z : ℂ | ‖p.eval z‖ ≤ 1}
    42→
    43→/--
    44→Erdős Conjecture (Problem #115) [Er61, Er90], proved by Eremenko–Lempert [ErLe94]:
    45→
    46→If $p(z)$ is a monic polynomial of degree $n$ such that $\{z \in \mathbb{C} : |p(z)| \leq 1\}$
    47→is connected, then
    48→$$\max \{ |p'(z)| : z \in \mathbb{C},\, |p(z)| \leq 1 \} \leq (1/2 + o(1))\, n^2.$$
    49→
    50→That is, for every $\varepsilon > 0$ there exists $N$ such that for all $n \geq N$, every
    51→monic polynomial $p$ of degree $n$ whose lemniscate is connected satisfies
    52→$|p'(z)| \leq (1/2 + \varepsilon)\, n^2$ for all $z$ in the lemniscate.
    53→
    54→Erdős originally conjectured the bound $n^2/2$ exactly (without the $o(1)$), but Szabados
    55→observed that the stronger form fails. Pommerenke [Po59a] proved the weaker bound
    56→$(e/2)\, n^2$. The monicness hypothesis is essential: without it, Chebyshev polynomials
    57→$T_n$ provide a counterexample, since $|T_n'(1)| = n^2$ while $\{z : |T_n(z)| \leq 1\}$
    58→is connected.
    59→-/
    60→@[category research solved, AMS 30]
    61→theorem erdos_115 :
    62→    ∀ ε : ℝ, 0 < ε →
    63→    ∃ N : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, N ≤ n →
    64→    ∀ p : Polynomial ℂ, p.Monic → p.natDegree = n →
    65→      IsConnected (lemniscate p) →
    66→      ∀ z : ℂ, z ∈ lemniscate p →
    67→        ‖(derivative p).eval z‖ ≤ (1 / 2 + ε) * (n : ℝ) ^ 2 := by
    68→  sorry
    69→
    70→end Erdos115
    71→

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 121
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/121](https://www.erdosproblems.com/121)
    23→
    24→Let $F_k(N)$ be the size of the largest subset of $\{1, \ldots, N\}$ with no $k$ distinct
    25→elements whose product is a perfect square. Erdős–Sós–Sárközy conjectured
    26→$F_{2k+1}(N) = (1 - o(1))N$ for $k \geq 2$; disproved by Tao, who showed $F_k(N) \leq
    27→(1 - c_k)N$ for all $k \geq 4$.
    28→
    29→[Er94b] [Er97] [Er97e] [Er98] Erdős, P., various problems papers.
    30→
    31→[ESS95] Erdős, P., Sárközy, A., and Sós, V. T., _On product representations of powers. I_.
    32→European Journal of Combinatorics (1995), 567-588.
    33→
    34→[Er38] Erdős, P., _On sequences of integers no one of which divides the product of two others
    35→and on related problems_. Tomsk. Gos. Univ. Ucen Zap. (1938), 74-82.
    36→
    37→[GrSo01] Granville, A. and Soundararajan, K., _The spectrum of multiplicative functions_.
    38→Annals of Mathematics (2) (2001), 407-470.
    39→
    40→[Ta24] Tao, T., _On product representations of squares_. arXiv:2405.11610 (2024).
    41→-/
    42→
    43→open scoped BigOperators
    44→
    45→open Filter
    46→
    47→namespace Erdos121
    48→
    49→/-- A finset $A$ has the property that no $k$ distinct elements have a product that is
    50→a perfect square. -/
    51→def NoKSquareProduct (k : ℕ) (A : Finset ℕ) : Prop :=
    52→  ∀ B : Finset ℕ, B ⊆ A → B.card = k → ¬IsSquare (∏ b ∈ B, b)
    53→
    54→/-- $F(k, N)$ is the size of the largest subset $A$ of $\{1, \ldots, N\}$ such that
    55→no $k$ distinct elements of $A$ have a product that is a perfect square. -/
    56→noncomputable def F (k N : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    57→  sSup {m : ℕ | ∃ A : Finset ℕ, A ⊆ Finset.Icc 1 N ∧ A.card = m ∧ NoKSquareProduct k A}
    58→
    59→/--
    60→Erdős Problem #121 [Er94b, Er97, Er97e, Er98] — DISPROVED
    61→
    62→Let $F_k(N)$ be the size of the largest $A \subseteq \{1,\ldots,N\}$ such that the product
    63→of no $k$ distinct elements of $A$ is a perfect square.
    64→
    65→Conjectured by Erdős, Sós, and Sárközy [ESS95]: Is $F_{2k+1}(N) = (1 - o(1)) N$ for all
    66→$k \geq 2$? Answered in the negative by Tao [Ta24].
    67→-/
    68→@[category research solved, AMS 5 11]
    69→theorem erdos_121 : answer(False) ↔
    70→    ∀ k : ℕ, 2 ≤ k →
    71→      Tendsto (fun N => (F (2 * k + 1) N : ℝ) / (N : ℝ)) atTop (nhds 1) := by
    72→  sorry
    73→
    74→/--
    75→Tao [Ta24] proved that for any $k \geq 4$ there exists a constant $c_k > 0$ such that
    76→$F_k(N) \leq (1 - c_k + o(1)) N$, disproving the conjecture for all odd $k \geq 5$.
    77→-/
    78→@[category research solved, AMS 5 11]
    79→theorem erdos_121.variants.tao_upper_bound :
    80→    ∀ k : ℕ, 4 ≤ k →
    81→    ∃ c : ℝ, 0 < c ∧
    82→      ∀ ε : ℝ, 0 < ε →
    83→        ∀ᶠ N : ℕ in atTop, (F k N : ℝ) ≤ (1 - c + ε) * N := by
    84→  sorry
    85→
    86→/--
    87→Erdős–Sós–Sárközy [ESS95] proved that $F_2(N) = (6/\pi^2 + o(1)) N$.
    88→That is, the largest subset of $\{1, \ldots, N\}$ with no two distinct elements whose product
    89→is a perfect square has asymptotic density $6/\pi^2$.
    90→-/
    91→@[category research solved, AMS 5 11]
    92→theorem erdos_121.variants.F2_asymptotic :
    93→    Tendsto (fun N => (F 2 N : ℝ) / (N : ℝ)) atTop (nhds (6 / Real.pi ^ 2)) := by
    94→  sorry
    95→
    96→/--
    97→Erdős–Sós–Sárközy [ESS95] proved that $F_3(N) = (1 - o(1)) N$.
    98→That is, almost all of $\{1, \ldots, N\}$ can be taken while avoiding three distinct elements
    99→whose product is a perfect square.
   100→-/
   101→@[category research solved, AMS 5 11]
   102→theorem erdos_121.variants.F3_asymptotic :
   103→    Tendsto (fun N => (F 3 N : ℝ) / (N : ℝ)) atTop (nhds 1) := by
   104→  sorry
   105→
   106→end Erdos121
   107→

    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 130
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/130](https://www.erdosproblems.com/130)
    23→
    24→Related to [Problem 213](https://www.erdosproblems.com/213).
    25→
    26→Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ be an infinite set with no three collinear and no four concyclic
    27→points. Can the integer-distance graph on $A$ (adjacent iff at integer distance) have infinite
    28→chromatic number?
    29→
    30→[Er97b] Erdős, P., _Some old and new problems in various branches of combinatorics_,
    31→Discrete Mathematics (1997), 227-231.
    32→
    33→[AnEr45] Anning, N.H. and Erdős, P., _Integral distances_, Bull. Amer. Math. Soc. (1945),
    34→598-600.
    35→-/
    36→
    37→open SimpleGraph EuclideanGeometry
    38→
    39→namespace Erdos130
    40→
    41→/-- A point set $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ is in Erdős general position if no three distinct
    42→points of $A$ are collinear, and no four distinct points of $A$ are concyclic. -/
    43→def ErdosGeneralPosition (A : Set ℝ²) : Prop :=
    44→  NonTrilinear A ∧
    45→  (∀ Q : Set ℝ², Q ⊆ A ∧ Q.ncard = 4 → ¬ EuclideanGeometry.Cospherical Q)
    46→
    47→/-- The integer-distance graph on a point set $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$: vertices are points
    48→of $A$, and two distinct points are adjacent if and only if their Euclidean distance
    49→is a positive integer. -/
    50→noncomputable def intDistGraph (A : Set ℝ²) :
    51→    SimpleGraph ↥A where
    52→  Adj p q := (p : ℝ²) ≠ q ∧
    53→    ∃ n : ℕ, 0 < n ∧ dist (p : ℝ²) (q : ℝ²) = n
    54→  symm := fun _p _q ⟨hne, n, hn, hd⟩ =>
    55→    ⟨hne.symm, n, hn, by rw [_root_.dist_comm]; exact hd⟩
    56→  loopless := fun _ ⟨hne, _⟩ => hne rfl
    57→
    58→/--
    59→Erdős Problem 130 [Er97b] (asked by Andrásfai and Erdős):
    60→Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ be an infinite set with no three points collinear and no four
    61→points concyclic. Consider the integer-distance graph $G$ on $A$, where two distinct points
    62→are adjacent if and only if their Euclidean distance is a positive integer.
    63→
    64→Can the chromatic number of $G$ be infinite?
    65→
    66→Note: The clique number is always finite. The Anning–Erdős theorem [AnEr45]
    67→shows that any infinite set of points in the plane with all pairwise integer
    68→distances must be collinear; since $A$ has no three collinear points, $A$ contains
    69→no infinite complete subgraph.
    70→-/
    71→@[category research open, AMS 5 51]
    72→theorem erdos_130 : answer(sorry) ↔
    73→    ∃ (A : Set ℝ²),
    74→      A.Infinite ∧ ErdosGeneralPosition A ∧
    75→      (intDistGraph A).chromaticNumber = ⊤ := by
    76→  sorry
    77→
    78→end Erdos130
    79→

    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→import FormalConjecturesForMathlib.Geometry.«2d»
    19→
    20→open scoped EuclideanGeometry
    21→
    22→/-!
    23→# Erdős Problem 135
    24→
    25→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/135](https://www.erdosproblems.com/135)
    26→
    27→Erdős and Gyárfás conjectured that any set of $n$ points in the plane where every
    28→$4$ points determine at least $5$ distinct distances must have $\gg n^2$ total distinct
    29→distances. This was disproved by Tao (2024). Erdős offered a $250 prize for this problem.
    30→
    31→[Er97b] Erdős, P., _Some old and new problems in various branches of combinatorics_,
    32→Discrete Mathematics (1997), pp. 227–231.
    33→
    34→[Er97e] Erdős, P., _Some of my favourite problems which recently have been solved_,
    35→Proc. Int. Conf. on Discrete Math. (1997), pp. 527–533.
    36→
    37→[Ta24c] Tao, T., _A counterexample to the Erdős–Gyárfás conjecture on distances_ (2024).
    38→-/
    39→
    40→namespace Erdos135
    41→
    42→/-- A finite point set $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ satisfies the "four-point, five-distance" property
    43→if every $4$-element subset determines at least $5$ distinct pairwise distances. -/
    44→def FourPointFiveDist (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) : Prop :=
    45→  ∀ S : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)),
    46→    S ⊆ A → S.card = 4 → 5 ≤ EuclideanGeometry.distinctDistances S
    47→
    48→/--
    49→Erdős–Gyárfás Conjecture (Problem 135) [Er97b, p. 231] [Er97e, p. 531]:
    50→Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ be a set of $n$ points such that any $4$ points determine at least
    51→$5$ distinct distances. Must $A$ determine $\gg n^2$ many distances?
    52→
    53→The conjecture asserts YES: there exists an absolute constant $c > 0$ such that
    54→every finite $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ with the four-point-five-distance property satisfies
    55→$|\text{distinct distances of } A| \geq c \cdot n^2$.
    56→
    57→This was answered in the NEGATIVE by Tao [Ta24c], who constructed for any
    58→large $n$ a set of $n$ points where any $4$ points determine at least $5$ distinct
    59→distances, yet the number of distinct distances is $O(n^2 / \sqrt{\log n})$.
    60→-/
    61→@[category research solved, AMS 5 52]
    62→theorem erdos_135 :
    63→    answer(False) ↔
    64→      ∃ c : ℝ, 0 < c ∧
    65→        ∀ A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)),
    66→          FourPointFiveDist A →
    67→          c * (A.card : ℝ) ^ 2 ≤ (EuclideanGeometry.distinctDistances A : ℝ) := by
    68→  sorry
    69→
    70→/--
    71→Stronger Erdős–Gyárfás Conjecture (Problem 135) [Er97b, p. 231]:
    72→Erdős made a stronger conjecture: any set $A$ of $n$ points in the plane with the
    73→four-point-five-distance property must contain a subset of $\gg n$ points where all
    74→pairwise distances are distinct. This is strictly stronger than `erdos_135` and is
    75→also false (disproved by Tao [Ta24c], since the weaker version already fails).
    76→-/
    77→@[category research solved, AMS 5 52]
    78→theorem erdos_135_strong :
    79→    answer(False) ↔
    80→      ∃ c : ℝ, 0 < c ∧
    81→        ∀ A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)),
    82→          FourPointFiveDist A →
    83→          ∃ B : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)),
    84→            B ⊆ A ∧
    85→            c * (A.card : ℝ) ≤ (B.card : ℝ) ∧
    86→            EuclideanGeometry.distinctDistances B = B.card * (B.card - 1) / 2 := by
    87→  sorry
    88→
    89→end Erdos135
    90→

    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→import FormalConjecturesForMathlib.Data.Set.Density

    20→/-!
    21→# Erdős Problem 144
    22→
    23→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/144](https://www.erdosproblems.com/144)
    24→
    25→[Er61, Er77c, Er79, Er79e, ErGr80, Er81h, Er82e, Er85e, Er97c, Er98] Erdős, P., various papers.
    26→
    27→[ErHa79] Erdős, P. and Hall, R. R., *The propinquity of divisors*, Bull. London Math. Soc.
    28→(1979), 304–307.
    29→
    30→[MaTe84] Maier, H. and Tenenbaum, G., *On the set of divisors of an integer*, Invent. Math. **76**
    31→(1984), 121–128.
    32→
    33→*Related problems:* [449](https://www.erdosproblems.com/449),
    34→[884](https://www.erdosproblems.com/884)
    35→
    36→*OEIS:* [A005279](https://oeis.org/A005279)
    37→-/
    38→
    39→namespace Erdos144
    40→
    41→/-- A positive integer $n$ has two divisors $d_1, d_2$ with $d_1 < d_2 < 2d_1$. -/
    42→def HasCloseDivisorPair (n : ℕ) : Prop :=
    43→  ∃ d₁ d₂ : ℕ, d₁ ∣ n ∧ d₂ ∣ n ∧ d₁ < d₂ ∧ d₂ < 2 * d₁
    44→
    45→/--
    46→Erdős Problem 144 [Er61, Er77c, Er79, Er79e, ErGr80, Er81h, Er82e, Er85e, Er97c, Er98]:
    47→The density of integers which have two divisors $d_1, d_2$ such that $d_1 < d_2 < 2d_1$
    48→exists and is equal to $1$.
    49→
    50→Formally, writing $A(N)$ for the number of integers $n$ with $1 \le n \le N$ which have
    51→two divisors $d_1 < d_2 < 2d_1$, we have $A(N)/N \to 1$ as $N \to \infty$.
    52→
    53→Proved by Maier and Tenenbaum [MaTe84].
    54→-/
    55→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    56→theorem erdos_144 :
    57→    {n : ℕ | HasCloseDivisorPair n}.HasDensity 1 := by
    58→  sorry
    59→
    60→/--
    61→Erdős Problem 144, generalized [MaTe84]:
    62→For any constant $c > 1$, the density of integers which have two divisors
    63→$d_1, d_2$ such that $d_1 < d_2 < c \cdot d_1$ exists and is equal to $1$.
    64→
    65→This is the stronger result proved by Maier and Tenenbaum, generalizing the
    66→original problem where $c = 2$.
    67→-/
    68→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    69→theorem erdos_144_generalized (c : ℝ) (hc : 1 < c) :
    70→    {n : ℕ | ∃ d₁ d₂ : ℕ, d₁ ∣ n ∧ d₂ ∣ n ∧ d₁ < d₂ ∧ (d₂ : ℝ) < c * d₁}.HasDensity 1 := by
    71→  sorry
    72→
    73→end Erdos144
    74→

    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 150
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/150](https://www.erdosproblems.com/150)
    23→
    24→A minimal vertex cut of a graph is a minimal set of vertices whose removal
    25→disconnects the graph. Let $c(n)$ be the maximum number of minimal vertex cuts a
    26→graph on $n$ vertices can have. Does $c(n)^{1/n} \to \alpha$ for some $\alpha < 2$?
    27→
    28→Asked by Erdős and Nešetřil, who also ask whether $c(3m+2) = 3^m$. Seymour
    29→observed that $c(3m+2) \geq 3^m$ via $m$ independent paths of length $4$ joining
    30→two vertices.
    31→
    32→Proved by Bradač [Br24]: the limit $\alpha = \lim c(n)^{1/n}$ exists and
    33→$\alpha \leq 2^{H(1/3)} = 1.8899\ldots < 2$, where $H(\cdot)$ is the binary entropy
    34→function. This confirms the conjecture. Bradač conjectures the true value is
    35→$\alpha = 3^{1/3}$.
    36→
    37→[Er88] Erdős, P., _Problems and results in combinatorics and graph theory_.
    38→
    39→[Br24] Bradač, D., _The number of minimal vertex separators_.
    40→-/
    41→
    42→open SimpleGraph Real Filter
    43→
    44→namespace Erdos150
    45→
    46→/-- A set $S$ of vertices is a vertex separator of $G$ if $S$ is a proper subset
    47→of $V$ and the subgraph induced by the complement $V \setminus S$ is not connected. -/
    48→def IsVertexSeparator {n : ℕ} (G : SimpleGraph (Fin n)) (S : Finset (Fin n)) : Prop :=
    49→  S ≠ Finset.univ ∧ ¬(G.induce ((S : Set (Fin n))ᶜ)).Connected
    50→
    51→/-- $S$ is a minimal vertex cut (minimal vertex separator) of $G$ if $S$ is a
    52→vertex separator and no proper subset of $S$ is also a vertex separator. -/
    53→def IsMinimalVertexCut {n : ℕ} (G : SimpleGraph (Fin n)) (S : Finset (Fin n)) : Prop :=
    54→  IsVertexSeparator G S ∧
    55→  ∀ T : Finset (Fin n), T ⊂ S → ¬IsVertexSeparator G T
    56→
    57→/-- The number of minimal vertex cuts of $G$. -/
    58→noncomputable def numMinimalVertexCuts {n : ℕ} (G : SimpleGraph (Fin n)) : ℕ :=
    59→  Set.ncard { S : Finset (Fin n) | IsMinimalVertexCut G S }
    60→
    61→/-- $c(n)$ is the maximum number of minimal vertex cuts over all connected simple
    62→graphs on $n$ vertices. -/
    63→noncomputable def c (n : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    64→  sSup { k : ℕ | ∃ G : SimpleGraph (Fin n), G.Connected ∧
    65→    numMinimalVertexCuts G = k }
    66→
    67→/--
    68→Erdős Problem 150 [Er88] (asked by Erdős and Nešetřil):
    69→Let $c(n)$ be the maximum number of minimal vertex cuts in a graph on $n$ vertices.
    70→Does $c(n)^{1/n} \to \alpha$ for some $\alpha < 2$?
    71→
    72→Proved by Bradač [Br24]: the limit $\alpha = \lim c(n)^{1/n}$ exists and
    73→$\alpha \leq 2^{H(1/3)} = 1.8899\ldots < 2$, where $H(\cdot)$ is the binary entropy
    74→function. Seymour's construction gives $c(3m+2) \geq 3^m$, so
    75→$\alpha \geq 3^{1/3} \approx 1.442$. Bradač conjectures that the true value is
    76→$\alpha = 3^{1/3}$.
    77→-/
    78→@[category research solved, AMS 5]
    79→theorem erdos_150 : answer(True) ↔
    80→    ∃ α : ℝ, α < 2 ∧
    81→      Tendsto (fun n : ℕ => (c n : ℝ) ^ ((1 : ℝ) / (n : ℝ)))
    82→        atTop (nhds α) := by
    83→  sorry
    84→
    85→/--
    86→Seymour's lower bound: the graph of $m$ independent paths of length $4$ joining
    87→two vertices shows $c(3m+2) \geq 3^m$.
    88→-/
    89→@[category research solved, AMS 5]
    90→theorem erdos_150_seymour_lower_bound (m : ℕ) :
    91→    c (3 * m + 2) ≥ 3 ^ m := by
    92→  sorry
    93→
    94→/--
    95→Erdős–Nešetřil exact conjecture: $c(3m+2) = 3^m$. This is stronger than the
    96→asymptotic statement of Problem 150 and remains open.
    97→-/
    98→@[category research open, AMS 5]
    99→theorem erdos_150_exact : answer(sorry) ↔
   100→    ∀ m : ℕ, c (3 * m + 2) = 3 ^ m := by
   101→  sorry
   102→
   103→/--
   104→Bradač's conjecture on the exact value of $\alpha$: the limit
   105→$\lim c(n)^{1/n} = 3^{1/3}$, matching Seymour's lower bound.
   106→-/
   107→@[category research open, AMS 5]
   108→theorem erdos_150_bradac_alpha : answer(sorry) ↔
   109→    Tendsto (fun n : ℕ => (c n : ℝ) ^ ((1 : ℝ) / (n : ℝ)))
   110→      atTop (nhds ((3 : ℝ) ^ ((1 : ℝ) / 3))) := by
   111→  sorry
   112→
   113→end Erdos150
   114→

    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 162
    21→
    22→For a 2-colouring of the edges of $K_n$, let $F(n, \alpha)$ be the smallest $m$ such that every
    23→subset of size at least $m$ has both colour classes containing more than an $\alpha$ fraction of its
    24→edges. Erdős conjectured that $F(n, \alpha) \sim c_\alpha \log n$ for a constant $c_\alpha > 0$.
    25→
    26→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/162](https://www.erdosproblems.com/162)
    27→
    28→[Er90b] Erdős, P., _Problems and results on graphs and hypergraphs: similarities and differences_.
    29→Mathematics of Ramsey theory, Algorithms and Combinatorics, 5 (1990), Springer, Berlin, 12-28.
    30→-/
    31→
    32→open Finset Real
    33→
    34→namespace Erdos162
    35→
    36→/-- A 2-colouring $c$ of the edges (2-element subsets) of $\operatorname{Fin}(n)$ is
    37→$\alpha$-balanced at threshold $m$ if for every subset $X \subseteq \operatorname{Fin}(n)$
    38→with $|X| \geq m$, each colour class contains more than $\alpha \binom{|X|}{2}$
    39→edges within $X$. -/
    40→def IsEdgeBalanced (n m : ℕ) (α : ℝ) (c : Finset (Fin n) → Bool) : Prop :=
    41→  ∀ X : Finset (Fin n), m ≤ X.card →
    42→    ∀ b : Bool,
    43→      α * (Nat.choose X.card 2 : ℝ) <
    44→        (((X.powersetCard 2).filter (fun S => c S = b)).card : ℝ)
    45→
    46→/-- $F(n, \alpha)$ is the smallest threshold $m$ such that there exists a 2-colouring
    47→of the edges of $K_n$ that is $\alpha$-balanced at threshold $m$. -/
    48→noncomputable def F (n : ℕ) (α : ℝ) : ℕ :=
    49→  sInf { m : ℕ | ∃ c : Finset (Fin n) → Bool, IsEdgeBalanced n m α c }
    50→
    51→/--
    52→Erdős Conjecture (Problem 162) [Er90b, p.21]:
    53→
    54→For every fixed $0 < \alpha < 1/2$, $F(n, \alpha) \sim c_\alpha \log n$ for some
    55→constant $c_\alpha > 0$. That is, the ratio $F(n, \alpha) / \log n$ converges to a positive
    56→constant $c_\alpha$ as $n \to \infty$.
    57→
    58→This sharpens the known bound $F(n, \alpha) = \Theta(\log n)$ (established via the
    59→probabilistic method) to an exact asymptotic with a well-defined constant.
    60→-/
    61→@[category research open, AMS 5]
    62→theorem erdos_162 :
    63→    ∀ α : ℝ, 0 < α → α < 1 / 2 →
    64→    ∃ c : ℝ, 0 < c ∧
    65→      ∀ ε : ℝ, 0 < ε →
    66→        ∃ N₀ : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, N₀ ≤ n →
    67→          (c - ε) * Real.log (n : ℝ) ≤ (F n α : ℝ) ∧
    68→          (F n α : ℝ) ≤ (c + ε) * Real.log (n : ℝ) := by
    69→  sorry
    70→
    71→end Erdos162
    72→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 175
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/175](https://www.erdosproblems.com/175)
    23→
    24→Show that, for any $n \geq 5$, the binomial coefficient $\binom{2n}{n}$ is not squarefree.
    25→
    26→It is easy to see that $4 \mid \binom{2n}{n}$ except when $n = 2^k$, and hence it suffices
    27→to prove this when $n$ is a power of $2$.
    28→
    29→Proved by Sárközy [Sa85] for all sufficiently large $n$, and independently by
    30→Granville and Ramaré [GrRa96] and Velammal [Ve95] for all $n \geq 5$.
    31→
    32→[Sa85] Sárközy, A., *On divisors of binomial coefficients, I*. J. Number Theory **20** (1985), 70-80.
    33→
    34→[GrRa96] Granville, A. and Ramaré, O., *Explicit bounds on exponential sums and the scarcity of squarefree binomial coefficients*. Mathematika **43** (1996), 73-107.
    35→
    36→[Ve95] Velammal, G., *Is the binomial coefficient $\binom{2n}{n}$ squarefree?*. Hardy-Ramanujan J. **18** (1995), 23-45.
    37→
    38→[Er79] Erdős, P., *Some unconventional problems in number theory*. Acta Math. Acad. Sci. Hungar. **33** (1979), 71-80.
    39→
    40→[ErGr80] Erdős, P. and Graham, R., *Old and new problems and results in combinatorial number
    41→theory*. Monographies de L'Enseignement Mathématique (1980).
    42→-/
    43→
    44→namespace Erdos175
    45→
    46→/--
    47→Erdős Problem 175 [Er79, p.67] [ErGr80, p.71]:
    48→For any $n \geq 5$, the central binomial coefficient $\binom{2n}{n}$ is not squarefree.
    49→That is, there exists some prime $p$ such that $p^2$ divides $\binom{2n}{n}$.
    50→-/
    51→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    52→theorem erdos_175 :
    53→    ∀ n : ℕ, 5 ≤ n → ¬Squarefree n.centralBinom := by
    54→  sorry
    55→
    56→end Erdos175
    57→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 185
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/185](https://www.erdosproblems.com/185)
    23→
    24→Let $f_3(n)$ be the maximal size of a subset of $\{0,1,2\}^n$ which contains no three
    25→points on a line. Is it true that $f_3(n) = o(3^n)$?
    26→
    27→Originally considered by Moser. It is trivial that $f_3(n) \ge R_3(3^n)$, the maximal
    28→size of a subset of $\{1,\ldots,3^n\}$ without a three-term arithmetic progression.
    29→Moser showed that $f_3(n) \gg 3^n / \sqrt{n}$. The answer is yes, which is a corollary of
    30→the density Hales-Jewett theorem, proved by Furstenberg and Katznelson [FuKa91].
    31→
    32→[Er73] Erdős, P., *Problems and results on combinatorial number theory*. A survey of
    33→combinatorial theory (Proc. Internat. Sympos., Colorado State Univ., Fort Collins, Colo.,
    34→1971) (1973), 117-138.
    35→
    36→[FuKa91] Furstenberg, H. and Katznelson, Y., *A density version of the Hales-Jewett
    37→theorem*. J. Anal. Math. 57 (1991), 64-119.
    38→
    39→OEIS: [A003142](https://oeis.org/A003142)
    40→-/
    41→
    42→namespace Erdos185
    43→
    44→/-- A combinatorial line in $\{0,1,2\}^n$ consists of three points parameterized by
    45→$j \in \{0,1,2\}$, such that there exists a nonempty set $S$ of "active" coordinates
    46→where the $j$-th point has value $j$ at each active coordinate, and all three points
    47→agree at non-active coordinates. -/
    48→def IsCombinatorialLine (n : ℕ) (p : Fin 3 → (Fin n → Fin 3)) : Prop :=
    49→  ∃ (S : Finset (Fin n)), S.Nonempty ∧
    50→    ∃ (c : Fin n → Fin 3),
    51→      ∀ (j : Fin 3) (i : Fin n),
    52→        p j i = if i ∈ S then j else c i
    53→
    54→/--
    55→Erdős Problem 185 (Density Hales-Jewett for $k=3$) [Er73]:
    56→
    57→For every $\varepsilon > 0$, there exists $N$ such that for all $n \ge N$, every subset
    58→$A \subseteq \{0,1,2\}^n$ of size $|A| > \varepsilon \cdot 3^n$ contains a combinatorial
    59→line (three points on a line).
    60→
    61→Equivalently, the maximum size $f_3(n)$ of a line-free subset of $\{0,1,2\}^n$ satisfies
    62→$f_3(n) = o(3^n)$.
    63→-/
    64→@[category research solved, AMS 5]
    65→theorem erdos_185 :
    66→    answer(True) ↔
    67→      ∀ ε : ℝ, 0 < ε →
    68→        ∃ N : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, N ≤ n →
    69→          ∀ A : Finset (Fin n → Fin 3),
    70→            (A.card : ℝ) > ε * (3 : ℝ) ^ n →
    71→            ∃ p : Fin 3 → (Fin n → Fin 3),
    72→              IsCombinatorialLine n p ∧ ∀ j : Fin 3, p j ∈ A := by
    73→  sorry
    74→
    75→end Erdos185
    76→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 192
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/192](https://www.erdosproblems.com/192)
    23→
    24→Let $A = \{a_1, a_2, \ldots\} \subset \mathbb{R}^d$ be an infinite sequence such that
    25→$a_{i+1} - a_i$ is a positive unit vector (i.e. of the form $(0,\ldots,1,\ldots,0)$). For which
    26→$d$ must $A$ contain a three-term arithmetic progression?
    27→
    28→This is equivalent to the following question about abelian squares: for which $d$ does every
    29→infinite word over a $d$-letter alphabet contain an abelian square (a pair of consecutive blocks
    30→that are permutations of each other)?
    31→
    32→The answer is: true for $d \le 3$, false for $d \ge 4$.
    33→
    34→[ErGr79] Erdős, P. and Graham, R., *Old and new problems and results in combinatorial number
    35→theory*.
    36→
    37→[ErGr80] Erdős, P. and Graham, R., *Old and new problems and results in combinatorial number
    38→theory*. Monographies de L'Enseignement Mathematique (1980).
    39→
    40→[Ke92] Keränen, V., *Abelian squares are avoidable on 4 letters*. Automata, languages and
    41→programming (Vienna, 1992), Lecture Notes in Comput. Sci. 623, Springer (1992), 41–52.
    42→
    43→[FiPu23] Fici, G. and Puzynina, S., *Abelian combinatorics on words: a survey*. Comput. Sci.
    44→Rev. (2023), Paper No. 100532.
    45→-/
    46→
    47→namespace Erdos192
    48→
    49→/-- An infinite word $w : \mathbb{N} \to \alpha$ contains an abelian square if there exist
    50→a starting position $i$ and a block length $n \ge 1$ such that the multiset
    51→of characters in positions $i, \ldots, i+n-1$ equals the multiset of characters
    52→in positions $i+n, \ldots, i+2n-1$. -/
    53→def HasAbelianSquare {α : Type*} (w : ℕ → α) : Prop :=
    54→  ∃ i n : ℕ, 0 < n ∧
    55→    (Finset.range n).val.map (fun j => w (i + j)) =
    56→    (Finset.range n).val.map (fun j => w (i + n + j))
    57→
    58→/--
    59→Erdős Problem 192, Part 1 [ErGr80]:
    60→Every infinite word over a 3-letter alphabet contains an abelian square.
    61→Equivalently, any infinite sequence in $\mathbb{R}^3$ with unit-vector steps must
    62→contain a three-term arithmetic progression.
    63→
    64→In fact, any finite word of length $\ge 7$ over $\{0,1,2\}$ contains an abelian square.
    65→-/
    66→@[category research solved, AMS 5]
    67→theorem erdos_192 :
    68→    ∀ w : ℕ → Fin 3, HasAbelianSquare w := by
    69→  sorry
    70→
    71→/--
    72→Erdős Problem 192, Part 2 [ErGr80]:
    73→There exists an infinite word over a 4-letter alphabet with no abelian square.
    74→Equivalently, there exists an infinite sequence in $\mathbb{R}^4$ with unit-vector steps
    75→that contains no three-term arithmetic progression.
    76→
    77→Proved by Keränen [Ke92].
    78→-/
    79→@[category research solved, AMS 5]
    80→theorem erdos_192.variants.four_letters :
    81→    ∃ w : ℕ → Fin 4, ¬HasAbelianSquare w := by
    82→  sorry
    83→
    84→end Erdos192
    85→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 205
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/205](https://www.erdosproblems.com/205)
    23→
    24→Erdős and Graham asked whether all sufficiently large $n$ can be written as $n = 2^k + m$ for some
    25→$k \geq 0$, where $m$ has fewer than $\log(\log(m))$ prime factors counted with multiplicity. This
    26→was disproved.
    27→
    28→See also Problem 851 for the related question using distinct prime factors.
    29→
    30→[ErGr80] Erdős, P. and Graham, R., *Old and new problems and results in combinatorial number
    31→theory*. Monographies de L'Enseignement Mathematique (1980).
    32→
    33→[Ro34] Romanoff, N. P., _Über einige Sätze der additiven Zahlentheorie_.
    34→Math. Ann. (1934), 668-678.
    35→-/
    36→
    37→open Real
    38→open scoped ArithmeticFunction.Omega
    39→
    40→namespace Erdos205
    41→
    42→/--
    43→Erdős Problem 205 (Disproved) [ErGr80, p.28]:
    44→
    45→Erdős and Graham asked whether all sufficiently large $n$ can be written as
    46→$2^k + m$ for some $k \geq 0$, where $\Omega(m) < \log(\log(m))$. Here $\Omega(m)$ is the number
    47→of prime divisors of $m$ counted with multiplicity.
    48→
    49→The answer is no (`answer(False)`): there are infinitely many counterexamples.
    50→-/
    51→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    52→theorem erdos_205 : answer(False) ↔
    53→    (∃ N : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, N ≤ n →
    54→      ∃ k : ℕ, 2 ^ k ≤ n ∧
    55→        (Ω (n - 2 ^ k) : ℝ) <
    56→          Real.log (Real.log ((n - 2 ^ k : ℕ) : ℝ))) := by
    57→  sorry
    58→
    59→/--
    60→Variant of Erdős Problem 205: whether the weaker bound $\Omega(m) < \varepsilon \log(\log(m))$
    61→for some $\varepsilon > 0$ suffices for all sufficiently large $n = 2^k + m$.
    62→-/
    63→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    64→theorem erdos_205_epsilon_variant : answer(sorry) ↔
    65→    (∃ ε : ℝ, 0 < ε ∧ ∃ N : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, N ≤ n →
    66→      ∃ k : ℕ, 2 ^ k ≤ n ∧
    67→        (Ω (n - 2 ^ k) : ℝ) <
    68→          ε * Real.log (Real.log ((n - 2 ^ k : ℕ) : ℝ))) := by
    69→  sorry
    70→
    71→/--
    72→Variant of Erdős Problem 205: whether there exist arbitrarily large *odd* integers $n$ such that
    73→for all $k$ with $2^k \leq n$, $\Omega(n - 2^k) \geq \log(\log(n - 2^k))$. Known counterexamples
    74→to the original conjecture are divisible by large powers of 2.
    75→-/
    76→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    77→theorem erdos_205_odd_variant : answer(sorry) ↔
    78→    (∀ N : ℕ, ∃ n : ℕ, N ≤ n ∧ Odd n ∧
    79→      ∀ k : ℕ, 2 ^ k ≤ n →
    80→        Real.log (Real.log ((n - 2 ^ k : ℕ) : ℝ)) ≤
    81→          (Ω (n - 2 ^ k) : ℝ)) := by
    82→  sorry
    83→
    84→end Erdos205
    85→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 215
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/215](https://www.erdosproblems.com/215)
    23→
    24→Does there exist a set $S \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ such that every set congruent to $S$
    25→contains exactly one point of $\mathbb{Z}^2$? The answer is yes, proved by Jackson and
    26→Mauldin [JaMa02] using the axiom of choice.
    27→
    28→[Er83c] Erdős, P., _Old and new problems in combinatorial analysis and graph theory_, 1983.
    29→
    30→[JaMa02] Jackson, S. and Mauldin, R.D., _On a lattice problem of H. Steinhaus_, Journal of the
    31→American Mathematical Society 15 (2002), 817-856.
    32→-/
    33→
    34→namespace Erdos215
    35→
    36→/--
    37→The integer lattice $\mathbb{Z}^2 \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$: points whose coordinates are all
    38→integers.
    39→-/
    40→def IntLattice : Set (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)) :=
    41→  {p | ∀ i : Fin 2, ∃ n : ℤ, p i = ↑n}
    42→
    43→/--
    44→Erdős Problem 215 (Steinhaus Problem):
    45→Does there exist $S \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ such that every set congruent to $S$
    46→(that is, $S$ after some isometry) contains exactly one point from $\mathbb{Z}^2$?
    47→
    48→The answer is yes, proved by Jackson and Mauldin [JaMa02].
    49→Their construction depends on the axiom of choice.
    50→-/
    51→@[category research solved, AMS 52]
    52→theorem erdos_215 : answer(True) ↔
    53→    ∃ (S : Set (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))),
    54→      ∀ (f : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2) → EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)),
    55→        Isometry f →
    56→        ∃! p, p ∈ f '' S ∧ p ∈ IntLattice := by
    57→  sorry
    58→
    59→end Erdos215
    60→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 225
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/225](https://www.erdosproblems.com/225)
    23→
    24→Let $f(\theta) = \sum_{k=0}^{n} c_k e^{ik\theta}$ be a trigonometric polynomial all of whose
    25→roots are real (equivalently, the polynomial $P(z) = \sum c_k z^k$ has all roots on
    26→the unit circle), with $\max_{\theta \in [0,2\pi]} |f(\theta)| = 1$. Then
    27→$\int_0^{2\pi} |f(\theta)| \, d\theta \leq 4$.
    28→
    29→This was proved independently by Kristiansen [Kr74] (for real $c_k$) and
    30→Saff and Sheil-Small [SaSh74] (for complex $c_k$). The original proof of
    31→Kristiansen contained an error which was later fixed in [Kr76].
    32→
    33→[Er40b, Er57, Er61] Erdős, P., original references.
    34→
    35→[Ha74] Hayman, W. K., _Research problems in function theory: new problems_ (1974), pp. 155–180,
    36→Problem 4.20.
    37→
    38→[Kr74] Kristiansen, G. K., _Proof of an inequality for trigonometric polynomials_.
    39→Proceedings of the American Mathematical Society (1974), pp. 49–57.
    40→
    41→[Kr76] Kristiansen, G. K., _Erratum to 'Proof of a polynomial conjecture'_.
    42→Proceedings of the American Mathematical Society (1976), p. 377.
    43→
    44→[SaSh74] Saff, E. B. and Sheil-Small, T., _Coefficient and integral mean estimates for algebraic
    45→and trigonometric polynomials with restricted zeros_. Journal of the London Mathematical
    46→Society (2) (1974/75), pp. 16–22.
    47→-/
    48→
    49→open Finset BigOperators MeasureTheory
    50→
    51→namespace Erdos225
    52→
    53→/-- A trigonometric polynomial: $f(\theta) = \sum_{k=0}^{n} c_k e^{ik\theta}$. -/
    54→noncomputable def trigPoly (n : ℕ) (c : ℕ → ℂ) (θ : ℝ) : ℂ :=
    55→  ∑ k ∈ range (n + 1), c k * Complex.exp (↑k * ↑θ * Complex.I)
    56→
    57→/--
    58→Erdős Problem 225 [Er40b, Er57, Er61, Ha74]:
    59→
    60→If $f(\theta) = \sum_{k=0}^{n} c_k e^{ik\theta}$ is a trigonometric polynomial whose
    61→associated algebraic polynomial $P(z) = \sum c_k z^k$ has all roots on the
    62→unit circle, and $\max_\theta |f(\theta)| = 1$, then
    63→$\int_0^{2\pi} |f(\theta)| \, d\theta \leq 4$.
    64→-/
    65→@[category research solved, AMS 42]
    66→theorem erdos_225 (n : ℕ) (c : ℕ → ℂ)
    67→    (hn : 0 < n)
    68→    (hlead : c n ≠ 0)
    69→    (hroots : ∀ z : ℂ, (∑ k ∈ range (n + 1), c k * z ^ k) = 0 → ‖z‖ = 1)
    70→    (hbound : ∀ θ : ℝ, ‖trigPoly n c θ‖ ≤ 1)
    71→    (hattained : ∃ θ : ℝ, ‖trigPoly n c θ‖ = 1) :
    72→    ∫ θ in (0 : ℝ)..(2 * Real.pi), ‖trigPoly n c θ‖ ≤ 4 := by
    73→  sorry
    74→
    75→end Erdos225
    76→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 240
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/240](https://www.erdosproblems.com/240)
    23→
    24→Is there an infinite set of primes $P$ such that if $\{a_1 < a_2 < \cdots\}$ is the set of
    25→positive integers divisible only by primes in $P$, then $\lim(a_{i+1} - a_i) = \infty$?
    26→
    27→Originally asked to Erdős by Wintner. The answer is yes for finite sets of primes,
    28→which follows from a theorem of Pólya [Po18].
    29→
    30→Tijdeman [Ti73] resolved this question, proving that for any $\varepsilon > 0$, there exists
    31→an infinite set of primes $P$ such that $a_{i+1} - a_i \gg a_i^{1-\varepsilon}$.
    32→
    33→See also: Problem 368.
    34→
    35→[Er61,p.226] Erdős, P., _Some unsolved problems_. Magyar Tud. Akad. Mat. Kutató Int. Közl.
    36→**6** (1961), 221–254.
    37→
    38→[Er65b] Erdős, P., _Extremal problems in number theory_. Proc. Sympos. Pure Math. **8** (1965),
    39→221–232.
    40→
    41→[Po18] Pólya, G., _Zur arithmetischen Untersuchung der Polynome_. Math. Z. (1918), 143–148.
    42→
    43→[Ti73] Tijdeman, R., _On integers with many small prime factors_. Compositio Math. (1973),
    44→319–330.
    45→-/
    46→
    47→namespace Erdos240
    48→
    49→/-- The set of positive integers all of whose prime factors lie in $P$. -/
    50→def smoothNumbers (P : Set ℕ) : Set ℕ :=
    51→  {n : ℕ | 0 < n ∧ ∀ q : ℕ, Nat.Prime q → q ∣ n → q ∈ P}
    52→
    53→/--
    54→Erdős Problem 240 [Er61,p.226][Er65b]:
    55→There exists an infinite set of primes $P$ such that if $\{a_1 < a_2 < \cdots\}$ is the
    56→set of positive integers divisible only by primes in $P$ (the $P$-smooth numbers),
    57→then the consecutive gaps $a_{i+1} - a_i$ tend to infinity.
    58→
    59→Equivalently: for every bound $B$, only finitely many $P$-smooth numbers $n$ have
    60→another $P$-smooth number within distance $B$ above them.
    61→
    62→Proved by Tijdeman [Ti73].
    63→-/
    64→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    65→theorem erdos_240 :
    66→    answer(True) ↔
    67→      ∃ P : Set ℕ, Set.Infinite P ∧ (∀ p ∈ P, Nat.Prime p) ∧
    68→        ∀ B : ℕ, Set.Finite {n : ℕ | n ∈ smoothNumbers P ∧
    69→          ∃ m ∈ smoothNumbers P, n < m ∧ m ≤ n + B} := by
    70→  sorry
    71→
    72→/--
    73→Tijdeman's quantitative strengthening of Erdős Problem 240 [Ti73]:
    74→For any $\varepsilon > 0$, there exists an infinite set of primes $P$ such that the
    75→consecutive gaps of $P$-smooth numbers satisfy $a_{i+1} - a_i \gg a_i^{1-\varepsilon}$.
    76→
    77→That is, there exists a constant $c > 0$ such that for all sufficiently large
    78→$P$-smooth numbers $n$, the next $P$-smooth number after $n$ is at least
    79→$n + c \cdot n^{1-\varepsilon}$.
    80→-/
    81→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    82→theorem erdos_240_tijdeman_quantitative :
    83→    ∀ ε : ℝ, ε > 0 →
    84→      ∃ P : Set ℕ, Set.Infinite P ∧ (∀ p ∈ P, Nat.Prime p) ∧
    85→        ∃ c : ℝ, c > 0 ∧ ∀ᶠ n in Filter.atTop,
    86→          n ∈ smoothNumbers P →
    87→            ∀ m ∈ smoothNumbers P, n < m → c * (n : ℝ) ^ (1 - ε) ≤ (m - n : ℝ) := by
    88→  sorry
    89→
    90→end Erdos240
    91→

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 256
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/256](https://www.erdosproblems.com/256)
    23→
    24→Let $n \geq 1$ and $f(n)$ be maximal such that for any integers
    25→$1 \leq a_1 \leq \cdots \leq a_n$,
    26→$$\max_{|z|=1} \left|\prod_i (1 - z^{a_i})\right| \geq f(n).$$
    27→
    28→Equivalently, $f(n)$ is the infimum over all non-decreasing sequences of positive
    29→integers $(a_1, \ldots, a_n)$ of the supremum of $\left|\prod_i (1 - z^{a_i})\right|$ over the
    30→unit circle.
    31→
    32→Erdős conjectured that there exists $c > 0$ such that $\log f(n) \gg n^c$ [Er61, Er64b].
    33→
    34→This specific conjecture was answered negatively by Belov and Konyagin [BeKo96],
    35→who proved that $\log f(n) \ll (\log n)^4$. The broader problem of precisely
    36→estimating $f(n)$ remains open.
    37→
    38→Related to the Chowla cosine problem (Erdős Problem #510).
    39→
    40→[Er61] Erdős, P., _Some unsolved problems_. Magyar Tud. Akad. Mat. Kutató
    41→Int. Közl. **6** (1961), 221–254.
    42→
    43→[Er64b] Erdős, P., _Problems and results on diophantine approximations_.
    44→Compositio Math. (1964), 52–65.
    45→
    46→[ErSz59] Erdős, P., Szekeres, G., _On the product $\prod_{k=1}^{n}(1-z^{a_k})$_.
    47→Académie Serbe des Sciences, Institut de Mathématiques (1959), 29–34.
    48→
    49→[At61] Atkinson, F. V., _On a problem of Erdős and Szekeres_. Canadian
    50→Mathematical Bulletin (1961), 7–12.
    51→
    52→[Od82] Odlyzko, A. M., _Minima of cosine sums and maxima of polynomials on the
    53→unit circle_. Journal of the London Mathematical Society (Series 2) (1982), 412–420.
    54→
    55→[BeKo96] Belov, A. S., Konyagin, S. V., _An estimate for the free term of a
    56→nonnegative trigonometric polynomial with integer coefficients_. Matematicheskie
    57→Zametki (Mathematical Notes) (1996), 627–629.
    58→
    59→[BoCh18] Bourgain, J., Chang, M.-C., _On a paper of Erdős and Szekeres_.
    60→Journal d'Analyse Mathématique (2018), 253–271.
    61→-/
    62→
    63→open Complex BigOperators Finset
    64→
    65→namespace Erdos256
    66→
    67→/-- The supremum of $\left|\prod_i (1 - z^{a_i})\right|$ as $z$ ranges over the unit circle,
    68→for a fixed sequence of exponents $a$. -/
    69→noncomputable def unitCircleMaxProd (n : ℕ) (a : Fin n → ℕ) : ℝ :=
    70→  sSup {y : ℝ | ∃ z : ℂ, ‖z‖ = 1 ∧
    71→    y = ‖∏ i : Fin n, (1 - z ^ (a i))‖}
    72→
    73→/-- $f(n)$ is the infimum over all non-decreasing sequences of positive integers
    74→$(a_1, \ldots, a_n)$ of the supremum of $\left|\prod_i (1 - z^{a_i})\right|$ on the unit
    75→circle. Equivalently, $f(n)$ is the largest real number such that for any positive
    76→integers $1 \leq a_1 \leq \cdots \leq a_n$, the maximum of
    77→$\left|\prod_i (1 - z^{a_i})\right|$ on the unit circle is at least $f(n)$. -/
    78→noncomputable def erdos256_f (n : ℕ) : ℝ :=
    79→  sInf {y : ℝ | ∃ (a : Fin n → ℕ),
    80→    (∀ i, 0 < a i) ∧ Monotone a ∧
    81→    y = unitCircleMaxProd n a}
    82→
    83→/--
    84→Erdős Conjecture (Problem 256) [Er61, Er64b]:
    85→
    86→Does there exist a constant $c > 0$ such that $\log f(n) \gg n^c$, i.e., do there exist
    87→constants $c > 0$ and $C > 0$ such that for all sufficiently large $n$,
    88→$$\log f(n) \geq C \cdot n^c?$$
    89→
    90→This was answered negatively by Belov and Konyagin [BeKo96], who showed
    91→$\log f(n) \ll (\log n)^4$.
    92→-/
    93→@[category research solved, AMS 11 30]
    94→theorem erdos_256 : answer(False) ↔
    95→    ∃ c : ℝ, 0 < c ∧
    96→    ∃ C : ℝ, 0 < C ∧
    97→    ∃ N₀ : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, N₀ ≤ n →
    98→      Real.log (erdos256_f n) ≥ C * (n : ℝ) ^ c := by
    99→  sorry
   100→
   101→/-- $f^*(n)$ is the variant of $f(n)$ where the exponents are required to be strictly
   102→increasing ($a_1 < \cdots < a_n$) rather than non-decreasing. Bourgain and Chang [BoCh18]
   103→proved $\log f^*(n) \ll (n \log n)^{1/2} \log \log n$. -/
   104→noncomputable def erdos256_fStar (n : ℕ) : ℝ :=
   105→  sInf {y : ℝ | ∃ (a : Fin n → ℕ),
   106→    (∀ i, 0 < a i) ∧ StrictMono a ∧
   107→    y = unitCircleMaxProd n a}
   108→
   109→/--
   110→Variant of Erdős Problem 256 with strictly increasing exponents:
   111→
   112→Bourgain and Chang [BoCh18] proved that $\log f^*(n) \ll (n \log n)^{1/2} \log \log n$,
   113→where $f^*(n)$ is defined as $f(n)$ but with the exponents required to be strictly
   114→increasing rather than non-decreasing.
   115→-/
   116→@[category research open, AMS 11 30]
   117→theorem erdos_256_strict : ∃ C : ℝ, 0 < C ∧
   118→    ∃ N₀ : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, N₀ ≤ n →
   119→      Real.log (erdos256_fStar n) ≤
   120→        C * ((n : ℝ) * Real.log n) ^ (1/2 : ℝ) * Real.log (Real.log n) := by
   121→  sorry
   122→
   123→end Erdos256
   124→

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 280
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/280](https://www.erdosproblems.com/280)
    23→
    24→Let $n_1 < n_2 < \cdots$ be an infinite sequence of integers with associated residue
    25→classes $a_k \pmod{n_k}$, such that for some $\varepsilon > 0$ we have
    26→$n_k > (1+\varepsilon)k \log k$ for all $k \geq 1$. Erdős and Graham conjectured that
    27→$$\#\{m < n_k : m \not\equiv a_i \pmod{n_i} \text{ for } 1 \leq i \leq k\} \neq o(k).$$
    28→
    29→This was disproved by Stijn Cambie, who observed that taking $n_k = 2^k$ and
    30→$a_k = 2^{k-1} + 1$ for $k \geq 1$ gives a trivial counterexample: the only $m$
    31→not in any congruence class is $1$, so the count is $1$ for all $k$.
    32→
    33→[ErGr80] Erdős, P. and Graham, R., *Old and new problems and results in combinatorial
    34→number theory*. Monographies de L'Enseignement Mathematique (1980).
    35→-/
    36→
    37→namespace Erdos280
    38→
    39→open Classical in
    40→/-- The count of integers $m < n(k)$ that avoid all congruence classes $a(i) \bmod n(i)$
    41→for $1 \leq i \leq k$. -/
    42→noncomputable def sieveCount (n a : ℕ → ℕ) (k : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    43→  ((Finset.range (n k)).filter fun m =>
    44→    ∀ i ∈ Finset.Icc 1 k, ¬(m ≡ a i [MOD n i])).card
    45→
    46→/--
    47→Erdős Problem 280 (Disproved) [ErGr80, p.29]:
    48→
    49→There exist a strictly increasing sequence $n_1 < n_2 < \cdots$ of positive integers
    50→and associated residue classes $a_k \pmod{n_k}$, with $n_k > (1+\varepsilon)k \log k$
    51→for some $\varepsilon > 0$ and all $k \geq 1$, such that the sieve count
    52→$$\#\{m < n_k : m \not\equiv a_i \pmod{n_i} \text{ for } 1 \leq i \leq k\}$$
    53→is $o(k)$ as $k \to \infty$.
    54→-/
    55→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    56→theorem erdos_280 :
    57→    ∃ (n : ℕ → ℕ) (a : ℕ → ℕ) (ε : ℝ),
    58→      0 < ε ∧
    59→      StrictMono n ∧
    60→      (∀ k, 1 ≤ k → (n k : ℝ) > (1 + ε) * ↑k * Real.log ↑k) ∧
    61→      ∀ c : ℝ, 0 < c →
    62→        ∃ K : ℕ, ∀ k, K ≤ k →
    63→          (sieveCount n a k : ℝ) < c * ↑k := by
    64→  sorry
    65→
    66→end Erdos280
    67→

    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 300
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/300](https://www.erdosproblems.com/300)
    23→
    24→[ErGr80] Erdős, P. and Graham, R., *Old and new problems and results in combinatorial number
    25→theory*. Monographies de L'Enseignement Mathematique (1980).
    26→
    27→[Cr03] Croot, E., *On a coloring conjecture about unit fractions*. Annals of Mathematics (2003).
    28→
    29→[Va99] Vardi, I., §1.14.
    30→
    31→[LiSa24] Liu, J. and Sawhney, M., *On a conjecture of Erdős and Graham about Egyptian
    32→fractions* (2024).
    33→-/
    34→
    35→open Finset BigOperators
    36→
    37→namespace Erdos300
    38→
    39→/-- A finset $A$ of positive integers is *Egyptian-1-free* if no nonempty subset
    40→$S \subseteq A$ has $\sum_{n \in S} 1/n = 1$. -/
    41→def Egyptian1Free (A : Finset ℕ) : Prop :=
    42→  ∀ S : Finset ℕ, S ⊆ A → S.Nonempty → ∑ n ∈ S, (1 : ℝ) / (n : ℝ) ≠ 1
    43→
    44→/--
    45→Erdős Problem 300 [ErGr80] [LiSa24]:
    46→
    47→Let $A(N)$ denote the maximal cardinality of $A \subseteq \{1, \ldots, N\}$ such that
    48→$\sum_{n \in S} 1/n \neq 1$ for all nonempty $S \subseteq A$. Estimate $A(N)$.
    49→
    50→Erdős and Graham believed $A(N) = (1 + o(1))N$. Croot [Cr03] disproved this, showing
    51→the existence of some constant $c < 1$ such that $A(N) < cN$ for all large $N$. The lower
    52→bound $A(N) \geq (1 - 1/e + o(1))N$ is trivial (a greedy argument gives an Egyptian-1-free
    53→set of this size).
    54→
    55→Liu and Sawhney [LiSa24] proved that $A(N) = (1 - 1/e + o(1))N$, resolving the problem.
    56→
    57→We formalize the resolved statement: for every $\varepsilon > 0$, for all sufficiently large $N$,
    58→(1) every Egyptian-1-free $A \subseteq \{1, \ldots, N\}$ has
    59→$|A| \leq (1 - 1/e + \varepsilon)N$, and
    60→(2) there exists an Egyptian-1-free $A \subseteq \{1, \ldots, N\}$ with
    61→$|A| \geq (1 - 1/e - \varepsilon)N$.
    62→-/
    63→@[category research solved, AMS 5 11]
    64→theorem erdos_300 :
    65→    ∀ ε : ℝ, 0 < ε →
    66→      ∃ N₀ : ℕ, ∀ N : ℕ, N₀ ≤ N →
    67→        (∀ (A : Finset ℕ), A ⊆ Finset.Icc 1 N → Egyptian1Free A →
    68→          (A.card : ℝ) ≤ (1 - Real.exp (-1) + ε) * (N : ℝ)) ∧
    69→        (∃ (A : Finset ℕ), A ⊆ Finset.Icc 1 N ∧ Egyptian1Free A ∧
    70→          (A.card : ℝ) ≥ (1 - Real.exp (-1) - ε) * (N : ℝ)) := by
    71→  sorry
    72→
    73→end Erdos300
    74→

    12→WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
    13→See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
    14→limitations under the License.

    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 320
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/320](https://www.erdosproblems.com/320)
    23→
    24→Let $S(N)$ count the number of distinct sums of the form $\sum_{n \in A} 1/n$
    25→for $A \subseteq \{1, \ldots, N\}$. Estimate $S(N)$.
    26→
    27→See also [Problem 321](https://www.erdosproblems.com/321) and
    28→[OEIS A072207](https://oeis.org/A072207).
    29→
    30→## Known Bounds
    31→
    32→Bleicher and Erdős proved:
    33→* **Lower bound** [BlEr75]: $\log S(N) \geq (N/\log N)(\log 2 \cdot \prod_{i=3}^{k} \log_i N)$
    34→* **Upper bound** [BlEr76b]: $\log S(N) \leq (N/\log N)(\log_r N \cdot \prod_{i=3}^{r} \log_i N)$
    35→
    36→where $\log_i$ denotes the $i$-fold iterated logarithm.
    37→
    38→Bettin, Grenié, Molteni, and Sanna [BGMS25] improved the lower bound to:
    39→$$\log S(N) \geq (N/\log N)(2 \log 2 (1 - 3/(2 \log_k N)) \cdot \prod_{i=3}^{k} \log_i N)$$
    40→
    41→[BlEr75] Bleicher, M. N. and Erdős, P., _The number of distinct subsums of ∑₁ᴺ 1/i_.
    42→Mathematics of Computation (1975), 29-42.
    43→
    44→[BlEr76b] Bleicher, M. N. and Erdős, P., _Denominators of Egyptian fractions. II_.
    45→Illinois J. Math. (1976), 598-613.
    46→
    47→[BGMS25] Bettin, S., Grenié, L., Molteni, G., and Sanna, C., _A lower bound for the number
    48→of Egyptian fractions_. arXiv:2509.10030 (2025).
    49→
    50→[ErGr80] Erdős, P. and Graham, R., _Old and new problems and results in combinatorial
    51→number theory_. Monographies de L'Enseignement Mathématique (1980).
    52→-/
    53→
    54→open Filter Real
    55→
    56→open scoped Finset
    57→
    58→namespace Erdos320
    59→
    60→/-- The set of all unit-fraction subset sums from $\{1, \ldots, N\}$:
    61→$\left\{ \sum_{n \in A} 1/n : A \subseteq \{1, \ldots, N\} \right\}$ as a finite set of
    62→rationals. -/
    63→noncomputable def unitFractionSums (N : ℕ) : Finset ℚ :=
    64→  (Finset.Icc 1 N).powerset.image
    65→    (fun A => A.sum (fun n => (1 : ℚ) / ↑n))
    66→
    67→/-- $S(N)$ counts the number of distinct sums $\sum_{n \in A} 1/n$
    68→for $A \subseteq \{1, \ldots, N\}$. -/
    69→noncomputable def erdos320_S (N : ℕ) : ℕ := (unitFractionSums N).card
    70→
    71→/--
    72→**Erdős Problem 320** [ErGr80, p. 43]:
    73→
    74→Estimate $S(N)$, the number of distinct sums of the form $\sum_{n \in A} 1/n$
    75→for $A \subseteq \{1, \ldots, N\}$.
    76→-/
    77→@[category research open, AMS 5 11]
    78→theorem erdos_320 (N : ℕ) :
    79→    erdos320_S N = answer(sorry) := by
    80→  sorry
    81→
    82→/-
    83→Formalisation note: it's possible that the solution to `erdos_320` needs to be
    84→expressed asymptotically. To handle this we include `IsTheta`, `IsBigO`
    85→and `IsLittleO` variants below. Since a solution is not known this necessitates
    86→the use of an `answer(sorry)` placeholder. Trivial or sub-optimal solutions
    87→will therefore exist to the asymptotic formalisations. A true solution to
    88→the asymptotic variants should have a degree of optimality or non-triviality to it.
    89→-/
    90→
    91→/--
    92→**Erdős Problem 320** (Theta variant):
    93→
    94→What is $\Theta(S(N))$?
    95→-/
    96→@[category research open, AMS 5 11]
    97→theorem erdos_320.variants.isTheta :
    98→    (fun N ↦ (erdos320_S N : ℝ)) =Θ[atTop] (answer(sorry) : ℕ → ℝ) := by
    99→  sorry
   100→
   101→/--
   102→**Erdős Problem 320** (Big-O variant):
   103→
   104→Find the simplest $g(N)$ such that $S(N) = O(g(N))$.
   105→-/
   106→@[category research open, AMS 5 11]
   107→theorem erdos_320.variants.isBigO :
   108→    (fun N ↦ (erdos320_S N : ℝ)) =O[atTop] (answer(sorry) : ℕ → ℝ) := by
   109→  sorry
   110→
   111→/--
   112→**Erdős Problem 320** (Little-o variant):
   113→
   114→Find the simplest $g(N)$ such that $S(N) = o(g(N))$.
   115→-/
   116→@[category research open, AMS 5 11]
   117→theorem erdos_320.variants.isLittleO :
   118→    (fun N ↦ (erdos320_S N : ℝ)) =o[atTop] (answer(sorry) : ℕ → ℝ) := by
   119→  sorry
   120→
   121→/--
   122→**Erdős Problem 320** (Known lower bound, Bleicher–Erdős 1975):
   123→
   124→$\log S(N) \geq (N / \log N) \cdot \log 2 \cdot \prod_{i=3}^{k} \log_i N$,
   125→valid for any $k \geq 4$ with $\log_k N \geq k$.
   126→
   127→[BlEr75] Bleicher, M. N. and Erdős, P., _The number of distinct subsums of ∑₁ᴺ 1/i_.
   128→Mathematics of Computation (1975), 29-42.
   129→-/
   130→@[category research solved, AMS 5 11]
   131→theorem erdos_320.variants.lower (N k : ℕ) (hk : 4 ≤ k) (hkN : k ≤ log^[k] N) :
   132→    N / log N * (log 2 * ∏ i ∈ Finset.Icc 3 k, (log^[i] N)) ≤
   133→      log (erdos320_S N : ℝ) := by
   134→  sorry
   135→
   136→/--
   137→**Erdős Problem 320** (Known upper bound, Bleicher–Erdős 1976):
   138→
   139→$\log S(N) \leq (N / \log N) \cdot \log_r N \cdot \prod_{i=3}^{r} \log_i N$,
   140→valid for any $r \geq 1$ with $\log_{2r} N \geq 1$.
   141→
   142→[BlEr76b] Bleicher, M. N. and Erdős, P., _Denominators of Egyptian fractions. II_.
   143→Illinois J. Math. (1976), 598-613.
   144→-/
   145→@[category research solved, AMS 5 11]
   146→theorem erdos_320.variants.upper (N r : ℕ) (hr : 1 ≤ r) (hrN : 1 ≤ log^[2 * r] N) :
   147→    log (erdos320_S N : ℝ) ≤
   148→      N / log N * (log^[r] N * ∏ i ∈ Finset.Icc 3 r, (log^[i] N)) := by
   149→  sorry
   150→
   151→/--
   152→**Erdős Problem 320** (Improved lower bound, BGMS 2025):
   153→
   154→$\log S(N) \geq (N / \log N) \cdot 2 \log 2 \cdot (1 - 3/(2 \log_k N)) \cdot
   155→\prod_{i=3}^{k} \log_i N$,
   156→valid for any $k \geq 4$ with $\log_k N \geq k$.
   157→
   158→[BGMS25] Bettin, S., Grenié, L., Molteni, G., and Sanna, C., _A lower bound for the number
   159→of Egyptian fractions_. arXiv:2509.10030 (2025).
   160→-/
   161→@[category research solved, AMS 5 11]
   162→theorem erdos_320.variants.lower_improved (N k : ℕ) (hk : 4 ≤ k) (hkN : k ≤ log^[k] N) :
   163→    N / log N * (2 * log 2 * (1 - 3 / (2 * log^[k] N)) *
   164→      ∏ i ∈ Finset.Icc 3 k, (log^[i] N)) ≤
   165→      log (erdos320_S N : ℝ) := by
   166→  sorry
   167→
   168→end Erdos320
   169→

    10→Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
    11→distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
    12→WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
    13→See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
    14→limitations under the License.
    15→-/
    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 340
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/340](https://www.erdosproblems.com/340)
    23→-/
    24→
    25→open Filter Finset
    26→open scoped Real Pointwise
    27→
    28→namespace Erdos340
    29→
    30→/-- Given a finite Sidon set `A` and a lower bound `m`, `go` finds the smallest number `m' ≥ m`
    31→such that `A ∪ {m'}` is Sidon. If `A` is empty then this returns the value `m`. Note that
    32→the lower bound is required to avoid `0` being a contender in some cases. -/
    33→private def greedySidon.go (A : Finset ℕ) (hA : IsSidon (A : Set ℕ)) (m : ℕ) :
    34→    {m' : ℕ // m' ≥ m ∧ m' ∉ A ∧ IsSidon (↑(A ∪ {m'}) : Set ℕ)} :=
    35→  if h : A.Nonempty then
    36→    haveI : ∃ m', m' ≥ m ∧ m' ∉ A ∧ IsSidon (↑(A ∪ {m'}) : Set ℕ) := by
    37→      simpa [and_assoc] using hA.exists_insert_ge h m
    38→    ⟨Nat.find this, Nat.find_spec this⟩
    39→  else ⟨m, by simp_all [IsSidon]⟩
    40→
    41→@[category test, AMS 5]
    42→theorem greedySidon_go_singleton_two : (greedySidon.go {1} (by simp [IsSidon]) 2).val = 2 := by
    43→  decide +native
    44→
    45→@[category test, AMS 5]
    46→theorem greedySidon_go_pair_three : (greedySidon.go {1, 2} (by simp [IsSidon]) 3).val = 4 := by
    47→  decide +native
    48→
    49→/-- Main search loop for generating the greedy Sidon sequence. The return value for step `n` is the
    50→finite set of numbers generated so far, a proof that it is Sidon, and the greatest element of
    51→the finite set at that point. This is initialised at `{1}`, then `greedySidon.go` is
    52→called iteratively using the lower bound `max + 1` to find the next smallest Sidon preserving
    53→number. -/
    54→private def greedySidon.aux (n : ℕ) : ({A : Finset ℕ // IsSidon (A : Set ℕ)} × ℕ) :=
    55→  match n with
    56→  | 0 => (⟨{1}, by simp [IsSidon]⟩, 1)
    57→  | k + 1 =>
    58→    let (A, s) := greedySidon.aux k
    59→    let s := if h : A.1.Nonempty then A.1.max' h + 1 else s
    60→    let s' := greedySidon.go A.1 A.2 s
    61→    (⟨A ∪ {s'.1}, s'.2.2.2⟩, s')
    62→
    63→/-- `greedySidon` is the sequence obtained by the initial set $\{1\}$ and iteratively obtaining
    64→then next smallest integer that preserves the Sidon property of the set. This gives the
    65→sequence `1, 2, 4, 8, 13, 21, 31, ...`. -/
    66→def greedySidon (n : ℕ) : ℕ := greedySidon.aux n |>.2
    67→
    68→@[category test, AMS 5]
    69→theorem greedySidon_zero : greedySidon 0 = 1 := rfl
    70→
    71→@[category test, AMS 5]
    72→theorem greedySidon_one : greedySidon 1 = 2 := by
    73→  decide +native
    74→
    75→@[category test, AMS 5]
    76→theorem greedySidon_two : greedySidon 2 = 4 := by
    77→  decide +native
    78→
    79→@[category test, AMS 5]
    80→theorem greedySidon_three : greedySidon 3 = 8 := by
    81→  decide +native
    82→@[category test, AMS 5]
    83→theorem greedySidon_four : greedySidon 4 = 13 := by
    84→  decide +native
    85→
    86→@[category test, AMS 5]
    87→theorem greedySidon_five : greedySidon 5 = 21 := by
    88→  decide +native
    89→
    90→@[category test, AMS 5]
    91→theorem greedySidon_ten : greedySidon 10 = 97 := by
    92→  decide +native
    93→
    94→/--
    95→Let $A = \{1, 2, 4, 8, 13, 21, 31, 45, 66, 81, 97, \ldots\}$ be the greedy Sidon sequence:
    96→we begin with $1$ and iteratively include the next smallest integer that preserves the
    97→Sidon property (i.e. there are no non-trivial solutions to $a + b = c + d$). What is the
    98→order of growth of $A$? Is it true that $|A \cap \{1, \ldots, N\}| \gg N^{1/2 - \varepsilon}$
    99→for all $\varepsilon > 0$ and large $N$?
   100→-/
   101→@[category research open, AMS 5]
   102→theorem erdos_340 (ε : ℝ) (hε : ε > 0) :
   103→    (fun n : ℕ ↦ √n / n ^ ε) =O[atTop]
   104→      fun n : ℕ ↦ ((Set.range greedySidon ∩ Set.Icc 1 n).ncard : ℝ) := by
   105→  sorry
   106→
   107→/--
   108→Let $A = \{1, 2, 4, 8, 13, 21, 31, 45, 66, 81, 97, \ldots\}$ be the greedy Sidon sequence:
   109→we begin with $1$ and iteratively include the next smallest integer that preserves the
   110→Sidon property (i.e. there are no non-trivial solutions to $a + b = c + d$). What is the
   111→order of growth of $A$? Is it true that $|A \cap \{1, \ldots, N\}| \gg N^{1/2 - \varepsilon}$
   112→for all $\varepsilon > 0$ and large $N$?
   113→-/
   114→@[category research open, AMS 5]
   115→theorem erdos_340.variants.isTheta (ε : ℝ) (hε : ε > 0) :
   116→    (fun n : ℕ ↦ ((Set.range greedySidon ∩ Set.Icc 1 n).ncard : ℝ)) =Θ[atTop]
   117→      (answer(sorry) : ℕ → ℝ) := by
   118→  sorry
   119→
   120→/--
   121→It is trivial that this sequence grows at least like $\gg N^{1/3}$.
   122→-/
   123→@[category undergraduate, AMS 5]
   124→theorem erdos_340.variants.third (ε : ℝ) (hε : ε > 0) :
   125→    (fun n : ℕ ↦ (n : ℝ) ^ ((1 : ℝ) / 3)) =O[atTop]
   126→      fun n : ℕ ↦ ((Set.range greedySidon ∩ Set.Icc 1 n).ncard : ℝ) := by
   127→  sorry
   128→
   129→/--
   130→Erdős and Graham [ErGr80] also asked about the difference set $A - A$ and whether this has
   131→positive density.
   132→
   133→[ErGr80] Erdős, P. and Graham, R., Old and new problems and results in combinatorial number
   134→theory. Monographies de L'Enseignement Mathematique (1980).
   135→-/
   136→@[category research open, AMS 5]
   137→theorem erdos_340.variants.sub_hasPosDensity :
   138→    Set.HasPosDensity (Set.range greedySidon - Set.range greedySidon) := by
   139→  sorry
   140→
   141→/--
   142→Erdős and Graham [ErGr80] also asked about the difference set $A - A$ and whether this
   143→contains $22$, which it does.
   144→
   145→[ErGr80] Erdős, P. and Graham, R., Old and new problems and results in combinatorial number
   146→theory. Monographies de L'Enseignement Mathematique (1980).
   147→-/
   148→@[category research solved, AMS 5]
   149→theorem erdos_340.variants._22_mem_sub :
   150→    22 ∈ Set.range greedySidon - Set.range greedySidon := by
   151→  sorry
   152→
   153→/--
   154→The smallest integer which is unknown to be in $A - A$ is $33$.
   155→ -/
   156→@[category research open, AMS 5]
   157→theorem erdos_340.variants._33_mem_sub : answer(sorry) ↔
   158→    33 ∈ Set.range greedySidon - Set.range greedySidon := by
   159→  sorry
   160→
   161→-- Formalisation note: there is some slight ambiguity in the meaning of
   162→-- "almost all" so we provide two variants for "all but finitely many"
   163→-- and "outside of a set of density zero"; there may be other reasonable
   164→-- interpretations
   165→/--
   166→It may be true that all or almost all integers are in $A - A$.
   167→-/
   168→@[category research open, AMS 5]
   169→theorem erdos_340.variants.cofinite_sub : answer(sorry) ↔
   170→    ∀ᶠ n in cofinite, n ∈ Set.range greedySidon - Set.range greedySidon := by
   171→  sorry
   172→
   173→/--
   174→It may be true that all or almost all integers are in $A - A$.
   175→-/
   176→@[category research open, AMS 5]
   177→theorem erdos_340.variants.co_density_zero_sub : answer(sorry) ↔
   178→    ∃ S : Set ℕ, S.HasDensity 0 ∧ ∀ n ∈ Sᶜ, n ∈ Set.range greedySidon - Set.range greedySidon := by
   179→  sorry
   180→
   181→end Erdos340
   182→

     7→
